turns.content: I literally have not slept more than 3 hours at a time in like a week and I'm losing my mind

That sounds really rough — a week of fragmented sleep will mess with everything. Are you waking up because of something specific (noise, pain, racing thoughts) or is it more like you just randomly pop awake and can't fall back asleep?

racing thoughts for sure. like I'll be so tired and then I lay down and my brain just starts going through everything, my patients from the shift, stuff I might have missed, things I need to do tomorrow, random anxiety spirals about nothing

That sounds like classic anxiety-driven insomnia — your body is exhausted but your brain won't power down. The work stuff especially makes sense because in healthcare the stakes feel so high that your mind keeps reviewing everything. Have you dealt with anxiety like this before, or is this more of a recent thing?

no I've had anxiety for years, I'm actually on sertraline for it and it usually helps but lately it's like the medication can't keep up?? especially with night shifts because my whole sleep cycle is already messed up and then the anxiety just makes it 10 times worse

That makes a lot of sense. Sertraline works well for a lot of people, but it can't override a completely disrupted circadian rhythm on its own. Night shift basically puts your body in a state of chronic stress, and anxiety meds have to fight even harder against that. Have you talked to your prescriber about how the sleep has gotten worse? They might want to adjust your dose or add something specifically for sleep.

I haven't because I keep putting it off, my doctor's office is only open during the day and that's when I'm sleeping so I always forget to call and then it's been another month. I know I know I need to just do it

The irony of a healthcare worker not having time for their own healthcare is painfully real. Can you set a phone alarm right now for whatever time you're usually awake — like 3 or 4pm — that says "CALL DOCTOR"? Just one specific alarm. A lot of offices also have online patient portals where you can message your doctor without calling. That might be easier since you can do it at 2am between patients.

oh my god the patient portal thing, why didn't I think of that. I literally use MyChart at work for my patients and never thought to message my own doctor on it. setting an alarm too but the portal message I can do right now honestly

Ha, it's always the obvious things you miss when you're running on empty! Yeah, send that message tonight if you can. Just tell them the sleep fragmentation has been going on for about a week, your anxiety is worse especially around shift times, and you want to discuss whether your sertraline dose needs adjusting or if there's something you can add for sleep. Short and specific so they can actually help.

ok I just drafted a message to send after this. in the meantime though is there anything I can do TONIGHT because I have a shift tomorrow night and I need to actually sleep today or I'm going to be a danger to my patients and that terrifies me

For today, here's a plan: when you get home from your shift, do a brain dump — write down every thought that's bugging you on paper or in your notes app, just get it out of your head. Then do something physically calming like a warm shower or some gentle stretching. Keep the room dark and cool. If your brain starts racing in bed, try the 4-7-8 breathing technique — inhale 4 seconds, hold 7, exhale 8. It sounds corny but it actually activates your parasympathetic nervous system. And no phone in bed — that one's huge.

the brain dump thing is interesting because I think part of my problem is I just carry everything from the shift home with me. like last night we had this 6 year old come in and she was so scared and I kept thinking about her after I left even though she's stable and being taken care of

That emotional carryover is one of the hardest parts of pediatric nursing. You care so much, which makes you great at your job, but it also means you take those kids home with you in your head. The brain dump can help create a boundary — like you're physically putting the shift down on paper and closing it. Some nurses I've heard of do a specific ritual when they leave the hospital, like taking off their badge and saying "I did my best today, they're in good hands." It sounds silly but it signals to your brain that the shift is over.

it doesn't sound silly at all actually. I think I need something like that because right now my "transition" from work to home is just driving home while still thinking about work and then laying in bed still thinking about work. there's no off switch

Exactly — you need to build that off switch because it won't happen on its own. Here's what I'd suggest: pick one specific moment on your drive home or when you walk in the door. Maybe it's when you take off your scrubs, maybe it's when you lock your front door. Make that your mental "I'm done" signal. After that point, if a work thought comes, you write it on the brain dump list and let it go. It takes practice but eventually your brain starts to learn the pattern.

I really like the scrubs thing because taking them off already feels like shedding the shift if that makes sense. ok so tonight's plan: brain dump, warm shower, scrubs-off ritual, 4-7-8 breathing, no phone in bed. and message my doctor about the sertraline. that's a lot but it's all manageable

That's a solid plan, and you don't have to nail all of it perfectly tonight — even doing two or three of those things will be better than what you've been doing. The doctor message is the most important long-term piece, so make sure that one happens. And hey, the fact that you're thinking about this and making a plan means you're already taking care of yourself. I hope you get some real rest today.

thank you honestly, I feel less panicky about it already just having a plan. ay dios mio I need sleep so bad. ok going to send that MyChart message and then try to get at least a few hours before tonight. wish me luck
Good luck! Send that message first, then do the warm shower and try to get some rest. You're going to take great care of your patients tonight, but first take care of you. Hope you sleep well.
